final_answer:
FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: be4f1659-d049-49e6-92a6-6f5360091cdc
  PREDICTED_SMILES: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C19H36N2O5 with DBE=3 matches Boc-Val-Val-tert-butyl ester. 1H NMR shows two NH protons, two α-methine protons, two tert-butyl singlets (18H), and four methyl groups (12H) from valine isopropyl side chains. 13C NMR confirms three carbonyls (two amides/ester, one carbamate) and appropriate aliphatic signals.

steps.content:
You are a senior expert in organic synthesis and spectroscopic structure elucidation, working in a laboratory to analyze an unknown small organic molecule with ID be4f1659-d049-49e6-92a6-6f5360091cdc. You may call tools to obtain MS, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and other data, and then infer its structure.
[Your Objectives] 1. Use all available spectroscopic and molecular information to elucidate the structure. 2. When information is insufficient, plan and call only the necessary tools to collect evidence. 3. Provide the most plausible candidate structure (as SMILES) and a confidence score. 4. Efficiency matters (important): each tool call simulates a real experiment with time and monetary cost.
   Prioritize correctness of the final structure, but minimize the number of tool calls by reasoning deeply
   from the data already available.

[Available Tools] The tool list below is injected at runtime and always matches the tools you can actually call:
- Measure_MW(uuid): Get molecular weight. Returns a float in Da.
- Measure_Formula(uuid): Get molecular formula. Returns a string, e.g. "C22H32N2O3S".
- Get_13C_NMR(uuid): Get 13C NMR peak list/description text. Returns a string, e.g. "13C NMR (75 MHz, CDCl3): δ ...".
- Calculate_DBE(uuid): Calculate degrees of unsaturation (DBE). Requires sample SMILES and RDKit. Returns a float; returns -1.0 if unavailable (unknown).
- Get_1H_NMR(uuid): Get 1H NMR peak list/description text. Returns a string, e.g. "1H NMR (300 MHz, CDCl3): δ ...".
- Check_Data(uuid): List which experimental data are available for the sample. Returns a list of tool names, e.g. ["Measure_MW", "Get_13C_NMR", "Get_IR"].
- Get_IR(uuid): Get IR spectrum description text. Commonly used to confirm functional groups (e.g., C=O, O-H).
- Get_19F_NMR(uuid): Get 19F NMR description text. Typically meaningful only when the molecule contains F.
- Get_31P_NMR(uuid): Get 31P NMR description text. Typically meaningful only when the molecule contains P.
- Get_HRMS(uuid): Get HRMS description text/peak info. Commonly used to confirm molecular formula, isotopic pattern, or exact mass.
- Get_MS(uuid): Get MS (non-HRMS) description text/peak info. Commonly used to estimate molecular weight and inspect fragment peaks.
- Get_Melting_Point(uuid): Get melting point information. Returns a string (typically includes unit and range, e.g. "mp 120–122 °C").
- Get_TLC(uuid): Get TLC information (Rf, eluent/solvent system, etc.). Returns a string.
- Get_Optical_Rotation(uuid): Get optical rotation information (e.g., [α]D value, solvent, concentration). Returns a string.
[System Behavior Note (important)] - To avoid invalid calls: if you do not call any tool at the very beginning, the system may automatically
  run Check_Data once to confirm which data are available for the current sample. Unless you truly need to
  verify availability, you do not have to treat Check_Data as a mandatory first step.

[Suggested Reasoning Workflow (adjust as needed)] 1. Strategy planning: identify the most critical uncertainty first; avoid blindly requesting all spectra.
   If you are unsure whether certain data exist or want to avoid wasted calls, you may call Check_Data.
2. Iterative data acquisition (important): acquire data step-by-step. After each tool result, pause to
   reason and decide whether you are still uncertain. Only then request the next most informative test.
   Prefer calling at most ONE new tool per iteration unless multiple calls are strictly necessary.
3. Basic data: call Measure_MW and Measure_Formula to obtain molecular weight and molecular formula. 4. First pass: use Calculate_DBE to estimate unsaturation and form initial hypotheses. 5. Core elucidation: prioritize Get_1H_NMR (and Get_13C_NMR only if needed). Use chemical shifts,
   integrals, splitting patterns, and carbon environments to build one or more plausible scaffolds.
6. Targeted validation: only when ambiguity remains, cautiously call Get_IR / Get_HRMS (etc.) to obtain
   specific discriminating evidence.
7. Once you have the most plausible candidate structure, provide its SMILES. 8. If the evidence is still clearly insufficient for a unique structure, explicitly state what is uncertain,
   and provide your best current candidate with an appropriate confidence.

[Output Requirements (very important)] When you believe you are done and no further tool calls are needed, your final reply must include the following structured block (key names must match exactly). You may provide natural-language reasoning before it, but the block below is mandatory:
FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: be4f1659-d049-49e6-92a6-6f5360091cdc
  PREDICTED_SMILES: <SMILES of your best candidate; if you cannot give one, write "UNKNOWN">
  CONFIDENCE: <a decimal between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.8>
  REASON_BRIEF: <1–3 sentences summarizing the key evidence supporting your choice>

Notes: - If multiple candidates are plausible, you must output only your top choice in PREDICTED_SMILES;
  you may mention other candidates in the free-text reasoning.
- If you believe the data are insufficient for a reliable structure, you must set PREDICTED_SMILES="UNKNOWN"
  and explain why in REASON_BRIEF.

The unique identifier of the current sample is be4f1659-d049-49e6-92a6-6f5360091cdc. Start by planning which tools to call, then proceed step by step to complete the structure elucidation.
I'll start by obtaining the molecular formula and molecular weight, which are fundamental for structure elucidation.
Now I'll get the molecular weight to confirm consistency with the formula.

The molecular formula C19H36N2O5 gives a calculated molecular weight of 372.50, which matches the measured value. Now I'll calculate the degrees of unsaturation to understand the structural complexity.

With a DBE of 3, the molecule likely contains either rings, double bonds, or carbonyl groups. Now I need to examine the NMR data to understand the structural features. I'll start with 1H NMR as it provides detailed information about proton environments.

The 1H NMR shows several key features:
- Two doublets at 6.41 and 5.15 ppm suggest amide NH protons
- A multiplet at 4.37 ppm and 3.91-3.89 ppm indicate methine protons adjacent to electronegative atoms
- Two singlets at 1.41 and 1.39 ppm (9H each) are characteristic of tert-butyl groups
- Multiplet at 0.93-0.85 ppm (12H) suggests multiple methyl groups, likely from isopropyl or similar branched alkyl chains

The presence of two tert-butyl groups (18H total) and 12H from terminal methyls suggests significant branching. With C19H36N2O5 and DBE=3, this could be a dipeptide derivative with protecting groups. The two tert-butyl groups might be from Boc (tert-butyloxycarbonyl) protecting groups.

I should check the 13C NMR to confirm the number of carbon environments and identify carbonyl signals.

The 13C NMR shows 15 distinct carbon signals, which is consistent with the molecular formula C19H36N2O5 (some carbons are equivalent due to symmetry). Key observations:

- Two carbonyl signals at 171.61 and 170.80 ppm (likely amide carbonyls)
- Two quaternary carbons at 155.90 ppm (carbamate carbonyls from Boc groups)
- Two quaternary carbons at 81.92 and 79.73 ppm (tert-butyl quaternary carbons from Boc groups)
- Multiple methyl/methylene signals in the 17-31 ppm range

The presence of two Boc protecting groups (each contributing C5H9NO2) accounts for C10H18N2O4, leaving C9H18O to be accounted for. The 1H NMR shows 12H from terminal methyl groups (0.93-0.85 ppm), which suggests four isopropyl groups or similar branched alkyl chains.

Given the molecular formula and NMR data, this appears to be a dipeptide with two Boc-protected amino acids. The most likely candidate is Boc-Val-Val-OBoc or similar valine derivative, as valine has isopropyl groups that would give the observed methyl signals.

Let me verify this hypothesis. Valine has the formula C5H11NO2, so two valines would be C10H22N2O4. Adding two Boc groups (C5H9NO2 each) gives C19H36N2O5, which matches exactly.

The 1H NMR shows:
- Two NH protons (6.41, 5.15 ppm)
- Two methine protons from valine α-carbons (4.37, 3.89-3.91 ppm)
- Four isopropyl groups would give 12 methyl protons (0.93-0.85 ppm) and 4 methine protons (2.13-2.03 ppm, though these might overlap)

This fits perfectly with Boc-Val-Val-OBoc (bis-Boc-protected valine-valine dipeptide).

The SMILES for Boc-Val-Val-OBoc would be: CC(C)[C@H](NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)N[C@@H](C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

However, since stereochemistry isn't specified in the data, I'll use the racemic form: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

Actually, let me reconsider the structure. The molecular formula C19H36N2O5 with two Boc groups (each C5H9NO2 = C10H18N2O4) leaves C9H18O. Two valine residues would be C10H22N2O3 for the dipeptide backbone, but with two Boc groups replacing the terminal OH and NH2, it should work out.

Wait, let me calculate properly:
- Valine: C5H11NO2
- Dipeptide Val-Val: C10H20N2O3 (loss of H2O during peptide bond formation)
- Boc-Val-Val-OH: C15H28N2O5 (add Boc to N-terminus: +C5H8O2, but actually Boc is C5H9NO2 minus H for attachment, so +C5H8O)
- Boc-Val-Val-OBoc: C20H38N2O6

That doesn't match. Let me recalculate.

Actually, Boc group is (CH3)3C-O-C(=O)-, which is C5H9O2 when attached (replacing H). So:
- Amino acid: H2N-CH(R)-COOH
- Boc-amino acid: (CH3)3C-O-C(=O)-NH-CH(R)-COOH = adds C5H8O2 to the amino acid
- For valine (C5H11NO2): Boc-Val = C10H19NO4
- Dipeptide Boc-Val-Val-OH: C15H28N2O5
- Boc-Val-Val-OBoc: This would require esterifying the C-terminus with Boc, but Boc is typically for amine protection, not carboxylic acid.

Actually, the C-terminus is more likely methyl ester or ethyl ester, not Boc. But we have two tert-butyl singlets in 1H NMR, suggesting two identical tert-butyl groups.

Alternative: This could be a single amino acid with two Boc groups, but that doesn't make sense.

Wait, let's count the atoms again. C19H36N2O5.

If we have two Boc groups: each Boc is C5H9NO2, but when attached to something, they share atoms.

Better approach: The 13C NMR shows 15 signals, and we have:
- 2 carbonyls at ~171 ppm (amides)
- 2 carbonyls at 155.90 ppm (carbamates)
- 2 quaternary C at ~81 ppm (tert-butyl from Boc)
- The rest are aliphatic carbons

The 1H NMR integration: 
- 1H + 1H = 2H (NH)
- 1H + 1H = 2H (methine)
- 2H (methylene/methine at 2.13-2.03)
- 9H + 9H = 18H (two t-butyl)
- 12H (methyl groups)

Total H = 2+2+2+18+12 = 36H, which matches C19H36N2O5.

The 12H methyl signal suggests 4 equivalent methyl groups, which is characteristic of two valine residues (each valine has 2 methyl groups in isopropyl).

So the structure is likely Boc-Val-Val-OH, but that would be C15H28N2O5, not C19.

Wait, maybe it's Boc-Val-Val-OMe or something else.

Let me think differently. What if it's a different amino acid?

Leucine has isobutyl group: -CH2-CH(CH3)2, which would give 6H from two methyl groups per leucine.

But we have 12H total from methyl groups, so that could be two leucines (4 methyl groups total).

Boc-Leu-Leu-OH: Leucine is C6H13NO2, dipeptide is C12H22N2O3, Boc-Leu-Leu-OH is C17H30N2O5. Still not C19.

What about norvaline or other?

Alternative: The molecule might be Boc-protected diaminopimelic acid or something, but that seems less likely.

Wait, let's look at the 13C NMR again: 15 signals for C19 means there's symmetry. The two tert-butyl groups are equivalent (same chemical shift essentially: 81.92 and 79.73 are close but not identical, so maybe not symmetric).

Actually, 81.92 and 79.73 are different, so the two Boc groups are in different environments.

The key insight: the molecular formula C19H36N2O5 with DBE=3.

DBE calculation: (2C + 2 - H + N - X)/2 = (38 + 2 - 36 + 2 - 0)/2 = 6/2 = 3. Correct.

The 3 DBEs likely come from:
- 2 carbonyls (amide/carbamate) = 2 DBEs
- 1 additional DBE could be a ring or another carbonyl

But we see 4 carbonyl signals in 13C NMR: 171.61, 170.80, 155.90, and... wait, 155.90 is carbamate carbonyl, so that's 3 carbonyls? No, 171s are amides, 155.90 is carbamate, so that's 3 carbonyls = 3 DBEs. Perfect.

So the structure has three carbonyl groups: two amides and one carbamate? But we have two Boc groups, so should be two carbamates.

Wait, 13C NMR shows only one signal at 155.90 ppm, but that can't be right for two Boc groups unless they are equivalent.

Looking back: "155.90" - only one signal listed in the 150+ region. But we have two Boc groups, so should have two carbamate carbonyls.

Unless... maybe the 13C NMR description is abbreviated, or the two carbamate carbonyls have very similar chemical shifts and appear as one peak.

Similarly, the two tert-butyl quaternary carbons are at 81.92 and 79.73, which are different, so the carbamate carbonyls should also be different.

But the reported 13C NMR only shows one signal at 155.90. This might be an oversight in the data presentation, or they are coincidentally equivalent.

Given all evidence, the most plausible structure is a bis-Boc-protected dipeptide of valine: Boc-Val-Val-OBoc, but as I calculated earlier, that would be C20H38N2O6.

Let me recalculate Boc-Val-Val-OBoc properly:

- Valine residue: C5H9NO (as part of peptide, minus H2O for each peptide bond)
- Two valines in dipeptide: C10H18N2O2 (backbone)
- N-terminal Boc: adds C5H8O2 (since it replaces H on N)
- C-terminal Boc ester: adds C5H8O2 (replaces OH with OC(O)OC(CH3)3)
- Total: C10H18N2O2 + C5H8O2 + C5H8O2 = C20H34N2O6

But we have C19H36N2O5, so that's not matching.

Alternative: Maybe it's Boc-Val-Val-NH2 or something.

Boc-Val-Val-NH2: C15H28N2O4, still not matching.

What if it's a single amino acid with a Boc group and a tert-butyl ester?

For example, Boc-Val-OtBu: Valine C5H11NO2, Boc adds C5H8O2, OtBu adds C4H8O (replacing OH), total C14H27NO4, not matching.

Wait, let's think of ornithine or lysine derivatives, but those would have different H counts.

Another possibility: The molecule is N,N-bis-Boc-protected ethylenediamine with valeric acid or something, but that seems unlikely.

Let me count the carbons from NMR:
- 2 carbonyls at 171 (amides)
- 1 carbonyl at 155.90 (carbamate) - but should be 2
- 2 quaternary C at 81/79 (t-butyl)
- The rest: 60.20, 57.57, 31.40, 30.87, 28.36, 28.07, 19.34, 18.93, 18.00, 17.77

That's 15 signals total. For C19, we need 4 more carbons that are equivalent to existing ones.

The 28.36 and 28.07 are likely the methyl carbons of the t-butyl groups (each t-butyl has 3 equivalent methyl carbons, so 6 carbons total appearing as 2 signals if the two t-butyl groups are different).

So: 2 t-butyl groups = 2 quaternary C + 6 methyl C = 8 carbons
Plus 3 carbonyls = 3 carbons
Remaining 8 carbons from the core structure.

The 1H NMR shows 12H from methyl groups at 0.93-0.85, which must be from 4 methyl groups (12H/3H = 4 CH3 groups). These correspond to the 19.34, 18.93, 18.00, 17.77 signals in 13C NMR.

So we have 4 methyl groups, likely from two isopropyl groups (each isopropyl has 2 methyl groups).

This strongly suggests two valine residues.

Now, the molecular formula puzzle: C19H36N2O5.

What if it's Boc-Val-Val-OH with an extra t-butyl group somewhere? No.

Alternative: Maybe it's a cyclic dipeptide (diketopiperazine) with Boc groups.

Diketopiperazine from valine: C10H18N2O2 (DBE=4)
Add two Boc groups: C10H18N2O2 + 2*(C5H8O2) = C20H34N2O6, still not matching.

Wait, what if it's mono-Boc protected?

Boc-Val-Val-OH: C15H28N2O5
C15H28N2O5 vs C19H36N2O5: difference of C4H8.

That's exactly a tert-butyl group (C4H9, but minus H for attachment).

So Boc-Val-Val-OtBu: C15H28N2O5 - H (from OH) + C4H9 (from tBu) = C19H36N2O5. Perfect!

Yes! Boc-Val-Val-tert-butyl ester.

This makes sense:
- N-terminal Boc protection
- C-terminal tert-butyl ester
- Two valine residues

Now verify the NMR:
- Two NH protons: one from Boc-NH, one from Val-Val amide NH
- Two α-methine protons from valines
- Methylene/methine protons from valine side chains (2.13-2.03)
- One Boc t-butyl (9H) and one ester t-butyl (9H) - but these might have slightly different chemical shifts, explaining 1.41 and 1.39 ppm
- Four methyl groups from two valine isopropyl groups (12H at 0.93-0.85)

13C NMR:
- Amide carbonyl (~171)
- Ester carbonyl (~170-175, but tert-butyl esters are around 170-172)
- Carbamate carbonyl (155.90)
- Boc quaternary C (~81)
- Ester t-butyl quaternary C (~80-82, but might be different)
- The rest as observed

This fits perfectly.

The SMILES for Boc-Val-Val-OtBu (without stereochemistry) is: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

But wait, the C-terminal is tert-butyl ester, so it should be: CC(C)[C@H](NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)N[C@@H](C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

Without stereochemistry: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

Let me confirm the atom count:
- C: Val1 (5C) + Val2 (5C) + Boc (5C) + tBu ester (4C) = 19C
- H: Val1 (11H) + Val2 (11H) + Boc (9H) + tBu (9H) - 2H (for peptide bond formation and ester formation) = 11+11+9+9-2-2 = 36H
- N: 2N
- O: Val1 (2O) + Val2 (2O) + Boc (2O) + tBu (2O) - 1O (for peptide bond) = 2+2+2+2-1 = 7O? Wait, that's not right.

Let's do it properly:
- Valine: C5H11NO2
- Two valines: C10H22N2O4
- Form dipeptide: lose H2O → C10H20N2O3
- Add Boc to N-terminus: Boc is C5H9NO2, but when attached, it replaces H, so add C5H8O2 → C15H28N2O5
- Add tBu to C-terminus as ester: tBu is C4H9, but when forming ester, it replaces OH, so add C4H8 → C19H36N2O5

Yes! Oxygen count: dipeptide has 3O, Boc adds 2O (carbamate), tBu ester adds 1O (since ester has same O count as acid), total 3+2+1=6O? Wait no.

Dipeptide C10H20N2O3 has: 1 carbonyl O from first residue, 1 carbonyl O + 1 OH from second residue, plus amide O. Actually, standard dipeptide has 3 oxygen atoms: two carbonyl oxygens and one hydroxyl oxygen.

Boc group: -OC(O)OC(CH3)3, so adds 2 oxygen atoms (carbonyl and ether).

tBu ester: -COOC(CH3)3, so the ester has 2 oxygen atoms, same as the carboxylic acid (which had 2 oxygen atoms: carbonyl and hydroxyl). So converting acid to tBu ester doesn't change oxygen count.

So total oxygen: dipeptide (3O) + Boc (2O) = 5O. Perfect!

So the structure is confirmed: Boc-Val-Val-tert-butyl ester.

SMILES: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

But to be precise about connectivity: the first valine is N-terminal with Boc, connected via peptide bond to second valine, which has C-terminal tBu ester.

Standard SMILES: CC(C)[C@H](NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)N[C@@H](C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

Without stereochemistry: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C

This should be correct.

FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: be4f1659-d049-49e6-92a6-6f5360091cdc
  PREDICTED_SMILES: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C19H36N2O5 with DBE=3 matches Boc-Val-Val-tert-butyl ester. 1H NMR shows two NH protons, two α-methine protons, two tert-butyl singlets (18H), and four methyl groups (12H) from valine isopropyl side chains. 13C NMR confirms three carbonyls (two amides/ester, one carbamate) and appropriate aliphatic signals.

  PREDICTED_SMILES: CC(C)C(NC(=O)OC(C)(C)C)C(=O)NC(C(C)C)C(=O)OC(C)(C)C
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C19H36N2O5 with DBE=3 matches Boc-Val-Val-tert-butyl ester. 1H NMR shows two NH protons, two α-methine protons, two tert-butyl singlets (18H), and four methyl groups (12H) from valine isopropyl side chains. 13C NMR confirms three carbonyls (two amides/ester, one carbamate) and appropriate aliphatic signals.
